preamble: The shift from the rhotic consonant [r] to the open central vowel [a] (or the near-open central [ɐ]) is a widespread form of r-vocalization. It typically occurs in syllable-final or post-vocalic positions where the rhotic articulation weakens into a vocalic residue, a process that defines the soundscape of non-rhotic English and modern German.

examples.note: The 'Tiefschwa' [ɐ] is the standard vocalized realization of final -er in German.
Loss of the rhotic consonant results in the lengthening and stabilization of the open back vowel.
